questions.prompt: A teammate says they will achieve full confidence by manually trying lots of inputs on a signup form until they run out of ideas. What is the core flaw in this plan?
options: Manual testing is always slower than automation
The input space is effectively infinite, so 'trying lots' still samples almost none of it and without a plan you can't say what you covered
Signup forms rarely contain bugs worth finding
You should only ever test the happy path first
questions.explanation: No amount of ad-hoc trying covers an effectively infinite input space, and without a design you have no record of what was actually exercised.

questions.prompt: Two testers each spend 15 minutes on the same form. One free-clicks 30 random attempts; the other runs 9 cases chosen by input class and edges. Which statement best captures the trade-off?
options: The 30 random attempts give better coverage because there are more of them
The 9 designed cases give broader, repeatable coverage with a record of what was tested
Both approaches give identical coverage since the time is equal
The designed cases are only better if the form has no bugs
questions.explanation: Designed cases deliberately spread across input classes and edges, so fewer steps reach more distinct behaviors and the run is repeatable and documented.

questions.prompt: A password field requires 8 to 20 characters. Which set of test inputs reflects design thinking rather than random clicking?
options: Empty, 7 chars, 12 chars, 21 chars (one per behavior class plus the edges)
Twenty random strings of assorted lengths
Only '12345678' because it is the minimum valid length
questions.explanation: Picking one representative per class and deliberately hitting the boundaries (7 and 21) probes distinct behaviors and off-by-one bugs; three valid strings all test the same class.

questions.prompt: During random clicking a tester sees the form crash, but cannot make it happen again. What does this episode most directly demonstrate about undesigned testing?
options: Crashes during testing should be ignored as flukes
Random clicking is unrepeatable, so failures are hard to reproduce and report
The crash proves the form has no real defects
Designed testing would have prevented the crash from occurring
questions.explanation: Without recorded, deliberate inputs you cannot reliably reproduce a failure; a designed case captures the exact inputs so the bug can be reproduced and filed.
